statement.markdown: Given the root of a binary tree, return the level order traversal of its nodes' values. (i.e., from left to right, level by level).The first line of the input contains a single integer which represents the number of nodes in the tree. Following that, each subsequent line contains a node value. Two nodes are connected if the second node is the left or right child of the first node. You need to return the level order traversal of the tree, with each level on a new line.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer n, the number of nodes in the tree. Then, there are n lines, each containing a node value. The parent-child relationship is implied by the order of the node values in the input, with the parent node appearing before its children.
statement.outputFormat: The output should be the level order traversal of the tree, with each level on a new line. The first line contains the number of levels in the tree, followed by each level on a separate line, with the number of nodes at that level on the first line of the level, followed by the node values.

constraints.description: The number of nodes in the tree.
The values of the nodes in the tree.

examples.explanation: The tree is     1    / \   2   3  / \   4   5The level order traversal is [1], [2, 3], [4, 5].
The tree is 1The level order traversal is [1].
The tree is     1    /   2The level order traversal is [1], [2].

hints.text: To solve this problem, you should first understand the structure of a binary tree and how nodes are connected. Think about how you can traverse the tree level by level.

A common approach to level order traversal is to use a data structure that allows you to efficiently add and remove nodes at both ends. Consider a data structure that follows the FIFO (First-In-First-Out) principle.
You will likely need to keep track of the nodes at each level and their corresponding values. Think about how you can separate the nodes into levels and process them accordingly.

editorial.title: Level Order Traversal of a Binary Tree
editorial.intuition: The problem requires traversing a binary tree level by level, from left to right. A brute force approach would involve recursively traversing the tree, but this would not naturally yield a level-by-level traversal.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve using a recursive function to traverse the tree. However, this would not be efficient for large trees and would not naturally produce the desired level-by-level traversal. The time complexity of such an approach would still be O(n), but it would involve more function calls and stack operations, making it slower in practice.
editorial.betterSolution: The key observation that unlocks the optimal approach is that a level order traversal can be easily achieved using a queue data structure. By keeping track of nodes at each level and processing them before moving on to the next level, we can efficiently traverse the tree level by level.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses a queue to perform a level order traversal of the binary tree. This approach has a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(n), where n is the number of nodes in the tree.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by induction on the number of levels in the tree. The base case is a tree with a single node, which is correctly traversed by the algorithm. For the inductive step, assume that the algorithm correctly traverses all trees with k levels. Then, for a tree with k+1 levels, the algorithm will correctly traverse the first k levels by the inductive hypothesis, and then process all nodes at level k+1 before moving on, thus correctly traversing the tree.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the algorithm on a sample tree confirms that it produces the correct level order traversal. For example, on a tree with nodes 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, the algorithm will correctly output [[1], [2, 3], [4, 5, 6]].
commonMistakes: Not using a queue data structure to keep track of nodes at each level.
Trying to use a recursive approach to traverse the tree level by level.
Not processing all nodes at each level before moving on to the next level.
Not handling the case where the tree is empty or has only one node.
